name: Carnaval de Binche
description: Recognized by UNESCO as a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity, featuring the 1000-strong march of the Gilles in elaborate outfits and wax masks.

name: Basler Fasnacht
description: A 24-hour celebration where performing groups called Cliques march through the streets, featuring the Morgenstreich procession in total darkness.

name: Up Helly Aa
description: Europe’s biggest fire festival celebrating Scotland’s Norse heritage, involving a replica Viking ship pulled through the streets and then set alight.

name: Carnaval de Dunkerque
description: A historic carnival for fishermen featuring surreal highlights like the singing of Cantate à Jean Bart and the mayor dropping 500kg of herring onto crowds.

name: Carnaval de Sitges
description: A week-long celebration that is a heart of Europe’s LGBT+ scene, featuring parades, parties, and the entierro de la sardina (burial of the sardine) parade.

name: Carnevale di Ivrea
description: A festival commemorating a Middle Ages revolt where townspeople engage in a massive battle using 500,000 pounds of oranges.

name: Shrovetide
description: A gathering where people wear costumes to beckon spring, featuring the torching of a winter symbol at the Rumšiškės folk museum.

name: Salto del Colacho
description: Known as the baby jumping festival, this Corpus Christi celebration involves men dressed as Satan leaping over newborns on mattresses to absolve them of evil.

name: La Pourcailhade
description: An annual festival celebrating pigs, featuring pig-squealing championships, piglet racing, and black pudding-eating competitions.

name: Busójárás
description: A six-day carnival where participants wear cloaks and horned masks to scare away winter, culminating in Carnival Sunday (Farsangvasárnap).
